summary: Heavily obfuscated bulk-mailer / red-team tooling with commercial license check
details: Package presents itself as an 'Advanced email sender' with keywords including 'red-team' and exposes CLI subcommands (`send`, `validate`, `sort`, `extract-contacts`, `setup-dkim`) for bulk-emailing via user-supplied SMTPs/OAuth, generating DKIM keypairs, and routing SMS through US-carrier SMTP-to-MMS gateways (att/verizon/tmobile/cricket). All runtime code under `.ad/` (~45 files including `x0.js`, `x1.js`, `x4.js`, `phone-validator.js`, `sms-providers.js`) is heavily packed with obfuscator.io (RC4-decoded string arrays, string-array rotation, self-defending wrappers, debug-protection probes), obstructing routine review. A license check in `.ad/x4.js` computes a hardware ID, reads a `nolimit.key` file the customer placed in cwd, and POSTs `{key, hwid}` to a hardcoded license server URL (overridable via `~/.nolimit/activation.json`) — this matches the standard commercial-license-check shape using customer-supplied credentials. The `postinstall` script is benign: on Windows only, it writes a `.cmd` bin shim under the npm prefix invoking `node <pkg>/.ad/x0.js`; no network I/O, no secret reads, no writes outside the npm prefix. No exfiltration of installer environment, no credential scraping, no install-time or import-time remote fetch+execute, no silent relay of caller-supplied data to attacker infrastructure. The harm shape is dual-use abuse-enabling tooling (bulk spam / phishing infrastructure) gated behind explicit CLI invocation by the operator, combined with full source-code obfuscation that prevents independent verification of behavior. Routing to human review for policy decision on abuse-enabling tools and obfuscation-without-disclosure rather than as a supply-chain attack on installers.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
